schools.summary: Joseph brought down to Egypt and bought by Potiphar: 'and Joseph was brought down to Egypt; and Potiphar, an officer of Pharaoh, captain of the guard, an Egyptian, bought him of the hands of the Ishmeelites, which had brought him down thither' — Joseph, sold by his brethren (37:28), carried down to Egypt (the resuming of the Joseph narrative, after the Judah interlude, ch. 38, the providence bringing Joseph to Egypt, where God would raise him to save his house, 45:5-8, the rejected son in a strange land); Potiphar (an officer of Pharaoh, captain of the guard, the executioner/bodyguard) buying Joseph from the Ishmaelites (37:28, 36, the Egyptian master, the providence placing Joseph in a great house, the slave bought into Potiphar's service); Joseph sold and bought (a slave), yet the providence of God overruling (the LORD with him, v.2, the descent that was the way to exaltation, the rejected son, by God's hand, brought to the place of his future glory, and the saving of many, 50:20) — Gill, Henry, Clarke, Calvin.

themes: And Joseph was brought down to Egypt; and Potiphar... captain of the guard, an Egyptian, bought him of the hands of the Ishmeelites
Joseph brought down to Egypt and bought by Potiphar
the providence bringing the sold son (37:28) to the place of his future glory
the descent that was the way to exaltation (45:5-8; 50:20)

schools.summary: The LORD with Joseph in his servitude: 'and the LORD was with Joseph, and he was a prosperous man; and he was in the house of his master the Egyptian' — the great recurring theme — 'the LORD was with Joseph': God's gracious presence with the enslaved Joseph (the phrase repeated, vv.3, 21, 23, the keynote of the chapter and of Joseph's life, Acts 7:9, 'God was with him,' not that Joseph's circumstances were easy, he was a slave, far from home, falsely accused, imprisoned, but that God was with him in them all — blessing, prospering, comforting, and protecting, the secret of his success and of his endurance, 'it is God's presence that makes prosperous' — the LORD's being with him the source of all); Joseph 'a prosperous man' (God prospering him, in his service, in all he did, vv.3-5, the prosperity flowing from God's presence, not from favorable circumstances, but from the LORD with him, the blessing on the godly servant, even in slavery, the success that God gave); 'in the house of his master the Egyptian' (Joseph serving in Potiphar's house, the LORD with him even there, in a heathen house, a slave, the providence blessing him in the place of his servitude, the godly young man, away from home and the means of grace, yet not forsaken, God with him) — Gill, Calvin, Wesley.

debates.question: What does 'the LORD was with Joseph' signify?

sides.position: God's gracious presence with the enslaved, falsely-accused, imprisoned son (the keynote, vv.3, 21, 23; Acts 7:9) - blessing and prospering him in all, not easy circumstances but God with him in them; the secret of his success and endurance

themes: And the LORD was with Joseph, and he was a prosperous man; and he was in the house of his master the Egyptian
the LORD being with Joseph in his servitude (the crux of God's presence)
the keynote (vv.3, 21, 23; Acts 7:9) - God with the enslaved son, blessing him in all
not easy circumstances, but God with him in them; God's presence makes prosperous

schools.summary: Potiphar perceives that the LORD prospered Joseph: 'and his master saw that the LORD was with him, and that the LORD made all that he did to prosper in his hand' — Potiphar (a heathen) perceiving that the LORD (the God of Joseph) was with him (the blessing on Joseph so evident that even his pagan master saw it, the witness of the world to God's presence with the godly, as Laban owned of Jacob, 30:27, the divine favor manifest); 'the LORD made all that he did to prosper in his hand' (God giving Joseph success in all his work, the prospering hand of God, the blessing that made Joseph valuable to his master, the evident favor of God on the servant's labors); Potiphar's seeing the LORD with Joseph leading to his promotion (v.4, the providence raising Joseph even in slavery, the world owning God's blessing on his servant, the favor that prepared Joseph's advancement) — Gill, JFB, Calvin.

themes: And his master saw that the LORD was with him, and that the LORD made all that he did to prosper in his hand
Potiphar perceiving that the LORD prospered Joseph
the world's witness to God's presence with the godly (as Laban, 30:27)
the favor that led to Joseph's promotion (v.4)

schools.summary: Joseph made overseer of Potiphar's house: 'and Joseph found grace in his sight, and he served him: and he made him overseer over his house, and all that he had he put into his hand' — Joseph finding favor with Potiphar (who saw God's blessing on him, v.3), serving him personally, and being made overseer (steward) over the whole house (the rapid rise of the godly servant, the trust reposed in Joseph, the providence advancing him); Potiphar making Joseph steward over his house — the high trust (the management of the whole estate, the favor that the blessing of God had won, v.3); 'all that he had he put into his hand' (Potiphar entrusting everything to Joseph, the complete confidence, the steward over all, the providence preparing Joseph for greater rule, ch. 41, the faithful servant trusted with all) — Gill, Clarke.

themes: And Joseph found grace in his sight, and he served him: and he made him overseer over his house, and all that he had he put into his hand
Joseph made overseer of Potiphar's house
the trust won by God's blessing (v.3)
the providence preparing Joseph for greater rule (ch. 41)

schools.summary: The LORD blesses Potiphar's house for Joseph's sake: 'and it came to pass from the time that he had made him overseer in his house, and over all that he had, that the LORD blessed the Egyptian's house for Joseph's sake; and the blessing of the LORD was upon all that he had in the house, and in the field' — God blessing Potiphar's house (and all his substance) for the sake of Joseph (the godly servant in it, the blessing that flows to a household for the sake of the righteous, as to Laban for Jacob, 30:27; Obed-edom for the ark, 2 Samuel 6:11, the presence of God's servant a blessing to the house); the blessing coming 'for Joseph's sake' (the righteous a blessing to those around them, God blessing the heathen master for the sake of his servant, the favor of God overflowing); 'the blessing of the LORD was upon all... in the house, and in the field' (the blessing on the whole estate, indoors and out, the household and the husbandry, the abundant favor, the prospering of Potiphar through Joseph, the providence enriching the master for the servant) — Gill.

themes: And it came to pass from the time that he had made him overseer... that the LORD blessed the Egyptian's house for Joseph's sake
the LORD blessing Potiphar's house for Joseph's sake
the righteous a blessing to those around them (as 30:27; 2 Samuel 6:11)
the blessing on the whole estate, in house and field

schools.summary: Potiphar's complete trust and Joseph's comeliness: 'and he left all that he had in Joseph's hand; and he knew not ought he had, save the bread which he did eat. And Joseph was a goodly person, and well favoured' — Potiphar entrusting everything to Joseph, taking no thought for anything himself except the food he ate (leaving all the management to Joseph, the complete confidence in the faithful steward, the high trust, Joseph's integrity proven, Potiphar at ease, all in Joseph's hand); Potiphar concerning himself with nothing but his food — so entirely did he trust Joseph (the steward's full charge, the master's ease); 'Joseph was a goodly person, and well favoured' (Joseph handsome, in form and face, like his mother Rachel, 29:17, the comeliness that occasioned the temptation, v.7, Potiphar's wife casting her eyes on him, the note that prepares for the trial to come, the godly young man, fair and trusted, about to be tempted) — Gill, Calvin, Wesley.

themes: And he left all that he had in Joseph's hand... save the bread which he did eat. And Joseph was a goodly person, and well favoured
Potiphar's complete trust in Joseph and Joseph's comeliness
the faithful steward fully trusted (Joseph's integrity proven)
the comeliness (29:17) that occasioned the temptation (v.7)

schools.summary: The temptation by Potiphar's wife: 'and it came to pass after these things, that his master's wife cast her eyes upon Joseph; and she said, Lie with me' — Potiphar's wife, taken with Joseph's beauty (v.6), looking upon him with lust and soliciting him directly ('Lie with me,' the bold, shameless temptation, the trial of the young man, in a strange land, a slave, with the powerful mistress, the sin pressed upon him); the woman 'cast her eyes' (the lust of the eye, the beginning of the sin, as Eve, 3:6; David, 2 Samuel 11:2, the unchaste desire, the temptation arising from Joseph's comeliness, the snare); 'Lie with me' (the bold, direct solicitation to adultery, the powerful temptation, the mistress, the opportunity, the danger of refusing, the trial that proved Joseph's chastity and fear of God, vv.8-9, the great temptation resisted) — Keil and Delitzsch, Gill, Henry, Calvin.

themes: And it came to pass after these things, that his master's wife cast her eyes upon Joseph; and she said, Lie with me
the temptation by Potiphar's wife
the bold, shameless solicitation (the lust of the eye, as 3:6; 2 Samuel 11:2)
the trial that would prove Joseph's chastity and fear of God (vv.8-9)

schools.summary: Joseph's refusal and his appeal to his master's trust: 'but he refused, and said unto his master's wife, Behold, my master wotteth not what is with me in the house, and he hath committed all that he hath to my hand' — Joseph refusing the solicitation (v.7, the firm, immediate refusal, the chastity of the godly young man, the resisting of the temptation, despite the danger and the persistence, v.10, the steadfast refusal); Joseph's first reason — his master's trust: Potiphar knew not (took no account of) what was in the house, but had committed all to Joseph (v.6, to betray such trust would be base ingratitude and treachery, the appeal to the trust reposed in him, the faithfulness that would not wrong his master); Joseph's appeal to his master's complete trust (v.6) — the wrong of betraying it (the fidelity of the steward, the first ground of his refusal, the trust, leading to the higher ground, the sin against God, v.9, the honorable, faithful servant) — Gill, Clarke.

themes: But he refused, and said unto his master's wife, Behold, my master wotteth not what is with me in the house, and he hath committed all that he hath to my hand
Joseph's refusal and his appeal to his master's trust
the firm refusal, the wrong of betraying such trust (v.6)
the fidelity of the steward, leading to the higher ground (v.9)

schools.summary: Joseph's great refusal - 'sin against God': 'there is none greater in this house than I; neither hath he kept back any thing from me but thee, because thou art his wife: how then can I do this great wickedness, and sin against God?' — Joseph reasoning that his master had withheld nothing from him but his wife (to take her would be the basest treachery against the master who trusted him, the wrong against Potiphar, the betrayal of trust); the great word — 'how then can I do this great wickedness, and sin against God?': Joseph's chief and highest ground for refusing — not merely the wrong to his master (real as that was), nor the fear of man, but the fear of God: the act would be a 'great wickedness' and a 'sin against God' (Joseph seeing the adultery primarily as a sin against God, in whose sight it would be evil, Psalm 51:4, 'against thee, thee only, have I sinned,' the fear of God the true root of his chastity, the conscience that would not sin against God even in secret, with none to see, in a strange land, the noblest motive for purity — that all sin is against God, the godly young man choosing rather to suffer, and he did suffer, vv.20, than to sin); Joseph's 'sin against God' the model of true chastity and integrity — grounded in the fear of God, not in the fear of man or the chance of detection (the conscience awed by God's presence, the example for all, the secret of resisting temptation — to see it as sin against God, the noble refusal) — Gill, Wesley, JFB.

debates.question: What was the chief ground of Joseph's refusal?

sides.position: The fear of God - Joseph saw the adultery primarily as a 'great wickedness' and 'sin against God' (Psalm 51:4), the conscience awed by God's presence even in secret; the fear of God the true root of chastity, not the wrong to his master nor the fear of man

themes: There is none greater in this house than I... how then can I do this great wickedness, and sin against God?
Joseph's great refusal - 'sin against God' (the crux of his chastity)
the chief ground the fear of God, the adultery seen as sin against God (Psalm 51:4)
the conscience awed even in secret, the fear of God the true root of chastity

schools.summary: Joseph's steadfast resistance to the daily temptation: 'and it came to pass, as she spake to Joseph day by day, that he hearkened not unto her, to lie by her, or to be with her' — Potiphar's wife persisting in her solicitation, day after day, that Joseph hearkened not unto her (the continued, persistent temptation, the trial prolonged, the snare pressed repeatedly, Joseph's steadfastness under the daily assault); Joseph 'hearkened not' — he refusing not only to lie with her but even 'to be with her' (avoiding her company, the occasion of sin, the prudent avoidance, the steadfast resistance, the wisdom of fleeing the occasion, not parleying with the temptation); 'or to be with her' (Joseph avoiding even being in her company, lest the temptation prevail, the prudent shunning of the occasion of sin, the steadfast, watchful resistance, the example of resisting persistent temptation by avoiding it, 1 Corinthians 6:18, flee fornication) — Gill, Calvin, Wesley.

themes: And it came to pass, as she spake to Joseph day by day, that he hearkened not unto her, to lie by her, or to be with her
Joseph's steadfast resistance to the daily temptation
the persistent temptation steadfastly resisted
the prudent avoidance of the occasion of sin (1 Corinthians 6:18)

schools.summary: Joseph alone in the house, the occasion seized: 'and it came to pass about this time, that Joseph went into the house to do his business; and there was none of the men of the house there within' — Joseph going in to do his work (the steward's duties), and it happening that none of the household servants were there (the occasion, the empty house, that Potiphar's wife would seize, v.12, the providence, and the snare, Joseph attending to his duty); Joseph going in 'to do his business' — about his lawful work (the faithful steward, not seeking the occasion, but caught in it, the diligent servant); 'there was none of the men of the house there within' (the empty house, the others away, perhaps by the wife's contrivance, at a festival, v.14, the opportunity for the temptation, the moment the wife would use, v.12, the trial at its height, Joseph alone, none to see — the test of his fear of God, v.9) — Keil and Delitzsch, Gill, Calvin.

themes: And it came to pass about this time, that Joseph went into the house to do his business; and there was none of the men of the house there within
Joseph alone in the house, the occasion seized
the diligent steward caught in the empty house
the test of his fear of God with none to see (v.9), the snare the wife would use (v.12)

schools.summary: Joseph flees, leaving his garment: 'and she caught him by his garment, saying, Lie with me: and he left his garment in her hand, and fled, and got him out' — Potiphar's wife, seizing the occasion (v.11), laying hold of Joseph's garment and pressing her solicitation ('Lie with me,' the bold, physical assault, the temptation at its most violent, the snare at its height); Joseph 'left his garment in her hand, and fled' — rather than yield, Joseph fleeing, leaving his garment in her grasp (the prompt, decisive flight from the temptation, the wisdom of fleeing, not parleying, 1 Corinthians 6:18, 'flee fornication'; 2 Timothy 2:22, choosing the loss of his garment, and the danger of her wrath, over the loss of his innocence, the noble flight, the example of resisting temptation by flight); 'and fled, and got him out' (Joseph escaping the house, the decisive break from the sin, the chastity preserved, at the cost of the garment, which would be used to accuse him, vv.13-18, the godly young man fleeing the snare, trusting God with the consequences) — Gill, Wesley.

themes: And she caught him by his garment, saying, Lie with me: and he left his garment in her hand, and fled, and got him out
Joseph fleeing, leaving his garment
the noble flight from temptation (1 Corinthians 6:18; 2 Timothy 2:22)
choosing the loss of his garment over his innocence

schools.summary: The wife's malice turning at Joseph's flight: 'and it came to pass, when she saw that he had left his garment in her hand, and was fled forth' — Potiphar's wife, seeing Joseph fled and his garment in her hand, turning from lust to malice (the scorned woman's spite, the love, lust, turned to hatred, the occasion of her false accusation, vv.14-18, the garment now her weapon); the garment (left in flight, v.12) becoming the false evidence — the wife using it to accuse Joseph (as if he had assaulted her, vv.15-18, the innocent token turned to a lie, the spite of the rejected woman); 'and was fled forth' (Joseph's flight, his innocence, provoking the woman's malice, the scorned lust turning to revenge, as Amnon's, 2 Samuel 13:15, love to hatred, the false accusation to follow, vv.14-18, the godly man's integrity answered with slander) — Gill, Henry.

themes: And it came to pass, when she saw that he had left his garment in her hand, and was fled forth
the wife's malice turning at Joseph's flight
the scorned lust become revenge (as 2 Samuel 13:15)
the garment turned to false evidence (vv.14-18)

schools.summary: The wife's false accusation against Joseph: 'that she called unto the men of her house, and spake unto them, saying, See, he hath brought in an Hebrew unto us to mock us; he came in unto me to lie with me, and I cried with a loud voice' — the wife, turning her malice to slander, calling the household servants and accusing Joseph (the 'Hebrew,' with contempt, blaming even her husband, 'he hath brought in,' for bringing the Hebrew, the false charge — that Joseph had come to 'mock,' insult, abuse, them, the lie inverting the truth, she the tempter, he the innocent); the false accusation — that Joseph 'came in unto me to lie with me' (the very thing she had solicited, v.7, now laid on him, the lie reversing the roles, the slander against the innocent, the false charge of assault); 'I cried with a loud voice' (the wife claiming she had cried out, the proof, by law, of a woman's resistance, Deuteronomy 22:24, 27, the false witness, the garment, v.15, the supposed evidence, the slander framed to convict Joseph, the innocent accused) — Keil and Delitzsch, Gill, Clarke.

themes: That she called unto the men of her house... See, he hath brought in an Hebrew unto us to mock us; he came in unto me to lie with me, and I cried with a loud voice
the wife's false accusation against Joseph
the lie inverting the truth (she the tempter, he the innocent)
the pretended cry, the slander framed to convict (Deuteronomy 22:24)

schools.summary: The wife's lie about Joseph's garment: 'and it came to pass, when he heard that I lifted up my voice and cried, that he left his garment with me, and fled, and got him out' — the wife's fabricated story: that Joseph, hearing her cry, fled and left his garment (twisting the true fact, v.12, that he fled to escape her, into a proof of his guilt, the lie built on the garment, the false account to the servants, and to Potiphar, vv.17-18, the slander framed); the garment (left in Joseph's flight, v.12) the wife's evidence — twisted to prove he had assaulted her (and fled when she cried, the innocent token turned to a lie, the proof fabricated); 'and fled, and got him out' (the true fact, Joseph fled to escape sin, v.12, inverted into a sign of guilt, fleeing when caught, the slander complete, the innocent's flight used against him, the false witness) — Gill.

themes: And it came to pass, when he heard that I lifted up my voice and cried, that he left his garment with me, and fled, and got him out
the wife's lie about Joseph's garment
the true fact (Joseph's flight from sin, v.12) inverted into proof of guilt
the garment the fabricated evidence, the slander framed

schools.summary: The wife keeping the garment for Potiphar: 'and she laid up his garment by her, until his lord came home' — the wife keeping Joseph's garment (the false evidence, vv.12, 15) until Potiphar returned (the deliberate preservation of the proof, the slander prepared for the husband, the false accusation to be laid before Potiphar, vv.17-18); the wife keeping the garment as her evidence — the deliberate, calculated slander (the proof readied for Potiphar, the malice premeditated); 'until his lord came home' (the wife awaiting Potiphar to lay the charge, vv.17-18, the false accusation prepared, the innocent Joseph to be condemned, the slander brought to the husband) — Gill.

themes: And she laid up his garment by her, until his lord came home
the wife keeping the garment for Potiphar
the false evidence kept, the slander premeditated
the accusation prepared for Potiphar (vv.17-18)

schools.summary: The wife repeating the false charge to Potiphar: 'and she spake unto him according to these words, saying, The Hebrew servant, which thou hast brought unto us, came in unto me to mock me' — the wife repeating to Potiphar the false accusation she had made to the servants (v.14): that the Hebrew servant (with contempt, and blaming Potiphar for bringing him) had come to 'mock,' abuse, her (the slander laid before the husband, the lie repeated); the wife blaming Potiphar ('which thou hast brought') for bringing the Hebrew — shifting blame, aggravating the charge (the contemptuous 'Hebrew servant,' the slander aimed to provoke the husband); 'came in unto me to mock me' (the false accusation of assault, as v.14, the lie inverting the truth, she the tempter, v.7, the slander against the innocent Joseph, the charge laid to condemn him, v.19) — Gill.

themes: And she spake unto him according to these words, saying, The Hebrew servant, which thou hast brought unto us, came in unto me to mock me
the wife repeating the false charge to Potiphar
the contemptuous 'Hebrew servant,' the blame shifted to Potiphar
the lie inverting the truth (v.7), the charge to condemn Joseph (v.19)

schools.summary: The wife producing the garment as proof: 'and it came to pass, as I lifted up my voice and cried, that he left his garment with me, and fled out' — the wife completing her false account to Potiphar (as to the servants, v.15): that Joseph, when she cried, fled and left his garment (the garment, vv.12, 16, produced as the proof, the lie sealed with the false evidence, the slander complete before the husband); the false claim of crying out (v.14) — the pretended resistance (the lie repeated to Potiphar, the false witness); 'he left his garment with me, and fled out' (the garment the supposed evidence, the true flight from sin, v.12, inverted into proof of guilt, the slander sealed, the innocent Joseph condemned by the false proof, v.19, the lie complete) — Gill.

themes: And it came to pass, as I lifted up my voice and cried, that he left his garment with me, and fled out
the wife producing the garment as proof
the false evidence completing the lie (the true flight, v.12, inverted)
the innocent Joseph condemned by it (v.19)

schools.summary: Potiphar's wrath kindled by his wife's accusation: 'and it came to pass, when his master heard the words of his wife, which she spake unto him, saying, After this manner did thy servant to me; that his wrath was kindled' — Potiphar, hearing his wife's accusation (vv.17-18), being angry, his wrath kindled (against Joseph, believing the false charge, the master deceived by the lie, the anger that led to Joseph's imprisonment, v.20); the wife's words — 'after this manner did thy servant to me' (the false charge of assault, the slander that provoked Potiphar, the innocent servant accused, the lie believed); Potiphar's wrath (yet, the witnesses note, his comparatively mild punishment — prison, not death, v.20 — may suggest some doubt of Joseph's guilt, or restraint by God, the anger that brought Joseph low, the false accusation, the trial of his faith, the providence overruling even the injustice, the prison the way to the palace, ch. 41, the innocent suffering wrongfully, 1 Peter 2:19) — Gill, Henry, Calvin.

themes: And it came to pass, when his master heard the words of his wife... that his wrath was kindled
Potiphar's wrath kindled by his wife's accusation
the master deceived, the innocent Joseph condemned by the false charge
the providence overruling the injustice (the prison the way to the palace, ch. 41; 1 Peter 2:19)

schools.summary: Joseph cast into prison: 'and Joseph's master took him, and put him into the prison, a place where the king's prisoners were bound: and he was there in the prison' — Potiphar imprisoning Joseph (in the royal prison, where the king's prisoners were kept — the prison attached to the captain of the guard's house, 40:3, the innocent cast into prison on the false charge, vv.17-19, the godly young man suffering wrongfully, the further descent, from steward to prisoner); the royal prison (where state prisoners were held, 40:3, Joseph bound there, Psalm 105:18, 'whose feet they hurt with fetters: he was laid in iron,' the harsh imprisonment, the trial of the innocent); 'he was there in the prison' (Joseph in the prison, yet the LORD with him there, v.21, the depth of his trial, sold, enslaved, falsely accused, imprisoned, the providence overruling — the prison the place where Joseph would meet the butler and baker, ch. 40, and be brought to Pharaoh, ch. 41, the innocent suffering, by God's hand, on the way to exaltation, Psalm 105:17-22) — Keil and Delitzsch, Gill, Clarke, Calvin.

themes: And Joseph's master took him, and put him into the prison, a place where the king's prisoners were bound: and he was there in the prison
Joseph cast into prison
the innocent suffering wrongfully on the false charge (1 Peter 2:19; Psalm 105:18)
the prison the way to the palace (ch. 41), the LORD yet with him (v.21)

schools.summary: The LORD with Joseph in the prison: 'but the LORD was with Joseph, and shewed him mercy, and gave him favour in the sight of the keeper of the prison' — even in the prison, the LORD with Joseph (the keynote again, v.2, God's gracious presence in the depth of his trial, the comfort and mercy of God to the innocent sufferer, the 'but' marking the contrast, imprisoned, yet not forsaken — the LORD with him, the faithfulness of God to his servant); God 'shewed him mercy' (kindness, covenant-love) — comforting and helping Joseph in the prison (the mercy of God to the afflicted, the kindness that sustained him, the LORD's tender care); 'gave him favour in the sight of the keeper of the prison' (God giving Joseph favor with the prison-keeper, as with Potiphar, v.4, the LORD inclining the keeper's heart to Joseph, the providence raising Joseph even in the prison, to be trusted with the prisoners, v.22, the favor of God turning the prison to a place of trust) — JFB, Gill, Calvin, Wesley.

themes: But the LORD was with Joseph, and shewed him mercy, and gave him favour in the sight of the keeper of the prison
the LORD being with Joseph in the prison
God's gracious presence in the depth of his trial (the keynote, v.2), the innocent not forsaken
the mercy and favor that turned the prison to a place of trust (v.22)

schools.summary: Joseph made overseer of the prison: 'and the keeper of the prison committed to Joseph's hand all the prisoners that were in the prison; and whatsoever they did there, he was the doer of it' — the prison-keeper, by God's favor (v.21), entrusting all the prisoners and the management of the prison to Joseph (the rise of the godly servant even in prison, as in Potiphar's house, v.4, the trust reposed, Joseph the overseer of the prison); Joseph given charge of all the prisoners — the high trust (paralleling his stewardship, v.4, the providence raising him, the management of the prison in his hand); 'whatsoever they did there, he was the doer of it' (Joseph ordering all that was done in the prison, the complete charge, the providence preparing him, and placing him where he would meet the butler and baker, ch. 40, and be brought to Pharaoh, ch. 41, the faithful servant trusted in the prison) — Keil and Delitzsch, Gill.

themes: And the keeper of the prison committed to Joseph's hand all the prisoners that were in the prison; and whatsoever they did there, he was the doer of it
Joseph made overseer of the prison
the rise of the godly servant even in prison (as in Potiphar's house, v.4)
the providence preparing him (to meet the butler and baker, ch. 40)

schools.summary: The LORD prospering Joseph in the prison: 'the keeper of the prison looked not to any thing that was under his hand; because the LORD was with him, and that which he did, the LORD made it to prosper' — the keeper of the prison taking no thought for anything committed to Joseph (as Potiphar had left all to him, v.6, the complete trust, the keeper at ease, all in Joseph's hand, the faithful steward trusted in the prison); the reason — 'because the LORD was with him' (the keynote, vv.2, 21, God's presence the source of Joseph's success and the keeper's trust, the LORD with him in the prison, the faithful God); 'that which he did, the LORD made it to prosper' (God prospering all Joseph's work in the prison, as in Potiphar's house, v.3, the prospering hand of God, the LORD with him making him prosper, the providence sustaining and advancing Joseph, the prison the way to the palace, ch. 41, the godly servant prospered by God in the depth of his trial) — Gill.

themes: The keeper of the prison looked not to any thing that was under his hand; because the LORD was with him, and that which he did, the LORD made it to prosper
the LORD prospering Joseph in the prison
God's presence the source of his success even in prison (the keynote, vv.2, 21)
the providence sustaining the innocent on the way to exaltation (ch. 41)
